data.title: Housebuilding giant blames Budget for biggest drop in sales since Covid
data.description:
Britain’s largest housebuilder has suffered the biggest drop in the number of homes sold since the pandemic after buyers were spooked by Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

Vistry, which owns brands such as Bovis and Countryside, sold 9.7pc fewer homes last year with sales falling to 15,700 from 17,225 in 2024.

Greg Fitzgerald, the company’s chief executive, said uncertainty over the November Budget had “delayed the timing” of some sales with “a more subdued market” triggered by Ms Reeves’s tax and spending plans.

The months leading to the Budget were dominated by speculation over the potential property taxes that the Chancellor would bring in to plug a multibillion-pound black hole in the public’s finances.

A so-called mansion tax on expensive homes will hit properties worth more than £2m with a tax surcharge from 2028 onwards.

Vistry’s woes will also prove a headache for Labour as it strives to hit its goal of building 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

The Government has pledged to accelerate building plans to meet the target after a slow start left it behind schedule.

At Vistry, sales for future construction projects by the housebuilder – which teams up with housing associations and councils to build affordable homes – stand at around £4bn, compared with £4.4bn in the previous year.

The sales drop marks the largest decline since the depths of the Covid pandemic, when sales plunged by a third to 4,652 as lockdown wiped out the property market.

Shares in the housebuilder fell by nearly 8pc during trading, wiping off £52m.

However, the builder backed Labour’s affordable housing measures, which include billions of pounds of funding to boost partnership contracts over the next decade, saying it will buoy trading.

Mr Fitzgerald said: “Whilst market conditions remain uncertain in the near term, further benefits of our cost, productivity and mix enhancement initiatives will support the delivery of good year-on-year financial and strategic progress.”

It comes after Persimmon, another housebuilder, warned it did not expect “any material improvement” in the housing market this year despite the Government’s efforts.

The builder warned that inflation will remain high, while extra regulatory costs and landfill tax charges will slow down growth.

data.title: Reading is in decline. So how come Waterstones is growing?
data.description:
Across Britain, fewer and fewer people are reading books. The data are plain as day.

The number of children who read outside of school every day has halved over the past two decades, while the number of children who say they enjoy reading in their spare time is down by 36pc.

Among adults, reading numbers are also in freefall. In a YouGov survey last year, two in five people said they had not read or listened to a book in the past year – compared with around a quarter of adults in 2001.

For Waterstones, figures such as these would be expected to raise alarm bells. With the high street in crisis, you would think the decline of reading would put businesses such as Waterstones next in line to face the chop.

Yet the mood is anything but sombre at Britain’s biggest bookseller. In fact, Waterstones’ owners are so confident in the business that they are gearing up for a multibillion-pound stock market listing, with a float expected as soon as this summer.

This month, it emerged that Elliott Advisors, the company’s owner, had lined up advisers at Rothschild to work on the process.

The private equity group is preparing to list both Waterstones and its US cousin Barnes & Noble together as one business.

In a sign of just how well Waterstones appears to be doing, Elliott is understood to be leaning towards the London Stock Exchange for a debut of the combined company.

James Daunt, the bookseller’s lauded chief executive who also runs Barnes & Noble, is himself a Briton.

Daunt has previously described a stock market float of the company as “inevitable”. Banks are expected to be hired in the coming weeks. Elliott has so far declined to comment on the process.

All this begs the question: why is Elliott so confident in Waterstones when more and more of us are giving up on books?

Daunt insists that book sales remain strong, even if it is a smaller pool of people who are buying.

“Stories of declines in reading evidently do not correlate to book buying,” he says. “Publishers and independent booksellers, as well as ourselves, are all doing well in both the US and the UK.”

The figures back him up. The number of independent booksellers across the UK rose slightly last year, even as the high street was struck by a series of retail collapses and store closures.

Between them, Barnes & Noble and Waterstones made a profit of $400m (£300m) last year, with sales standing at $3bn.

That is despite Barnes & Noble facing the same problem with declining reading numbers as Waterstones. A study by the University of Florida in August found that the number of Americans reading for pleasure had plunged by 40pc over the past 20 years.

Official accounts show that Waterstones’ sales rose to £565.6m for the 12 months to May 3, compared with £528.4m a year earlier, according to documents published on Companies House this week. It made pre-tax profits of £40m for the year.

Daunt, who runs both booksellers, described 2025 as a “fantastic year for us”, with both the US and UK businesses expanding into new areas.

Barnes & Noble opened 67 new stores while Waterstone is adding around 10 new shops a year, even though expansion is harder given it is so well-established.

Daunt – who has run Waterstones since 2011 and Barnes & Noble since 2018 – puts his success down to “establishing principles and nudging things in the direction you want it to go”.

“We’re just simply trying to make it easier and better for the people in the shops,” he previously told The Telegraph.

This includes standardising guides to train up staff and giving managers “complete autonomy in terms of reordering of books and placement of books”. This allows them to more easily respond to the tastes of local readers.

Waterstones and Barnes & Noble may appear to be in rude health – but convincing investors to buy into the business when it joins the stock market may not be so straightforward.

Investors have been burned by private equity-backed companies that listed in London in the past. City veterans still talk ruefully about the class of 2021 – a group of businesses that performed hideously.

Online furniture business Made.com ultimately collapsed into administration, Dr Martens suffered a share price collapse and is today worth around a fifth of its float price and Deliveroo endured the worst debut in history, with shares tumbling as much as 30pc on the first day of trading.

London has seen very few new retail debuts on the stock market in the years since.

Investors will be wary of Elliott and its advisers overvaluing the company, particularly if it appears that much of the sales growth is coming from store openings rather than a growing market.

Anubhav Malhotra, a Panmure Liberum analyst, says investors are very aware that “past IPOs haven’t worked out too well”. Valuations have come down “across the board” in London as a result, especially among retail stocks.

Sources close to Waterstones downplay these challenges and point to the fact that there are plenty of retailers doing well on the London market, such as Next and M&S. They argue that there is room for growth with plenty of territory yet to be conquered by both chains.

Also, while reading may be declining overall, it continues to be hugely popular among certain groups.

Waterstones recently said it had been boosted by growing demand for “romantasy” novels – known colloquially as fairy porn.

These fantastical tales of heroines being swept off their feet by knights and wizards have gained huge numbers of fans among British women.

Sarah J Maas, author of romantasy novel A Court of Thorns and Roses, has sold more than 75 million copies of her books globally. Publisher Bloomsbury said demand for Maas’s books helped it to the highest first-half sales and earnings in its history last year.

Elliott is understood to be planning to remain as the biggest shareholder in the combined bookselling business for some time following the market debut, which may give new investors more confidence in its future.

One banking source suggested now was as good a time as any for Elliott to kick off the process of listing Waterstones on the stock market and realising some return on its investment.

“Everything is a bit s--t,” they said. “But that’s not going to change anytime soon and there have been so few returns back on private equity that if they can, they probably should.”

For Daunt, his focus continues to be on the day-to-day. Over the past week, he has been travelling down the west coast of the US to visit Barnes & Noble shops. Despite falling reader numbers across both the US and UK, Daunt says stores are thriving.

“Bookselling is presently vibrant,” he says.

Soon he will find out if investors agree.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: It’s true – new-build estates really do hurt your house price
data.description:
Do you own a home near a new development? We want to hear about your experience, good or bad. Email money@telegraph.co.uk*

So-called “Nimbys” have become a scapegoat, accused of standing in the way of Labour’s vision to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

But a Telegraph investigation reveals that they do so with good reason: new housing developments really do have a detrimental effect on the values of existing homes in the area.

House prices in the English countryside fell by £7,000 on average after major developments were built within half a mile, analysis shows, compared with a £3,000 rise for other homes in the area.

Rural homes within eyeshot of new developments decreased in value by 2.7pc over the past four years. By contrast, house prices in the same towns but further away grew by 1.3pc.

It comes as the Government introduces measures to speed up building in an attempt to meet its housing targets.

The homes that take a hit

The majority of Britons support, in principle, a large increase in the amount of new housing, according to a YouGov poll which found that 52pc were in favour compared with 37pc who were not.

However, when it comes to house building in their local area, that support turns to opposition.

In densely packed cities, residents generally understand that new housing projects bring necessary urban renewal. An ageing estate in poor condition may be torn down to make way for a modern development, or a disused industrial site turned into an affordable housing hub.

But it’s a different story in the countryside.

Opponents of building projects – often referred to as Nimbys, which stands for “not in my backyard” – believe that they spoil the British countryside, disturb the bucolic quiet, damage existing homeowners’ views or quality of life and put strain on local services and amenities. They argue that their property prices will fall if their rural homes become less desirable because of nearby modern developments and busier local populations.

The Telegraph’s analysis, using official transaction data from HM Land Registry, looked at new-build sales in villages and towns with a population of below 20,000.

It included only areas with a minimum of 50 new housing transactions in 2024 that were within a quarter-mile radius of each other to ensure that the housing project was significant enough to change the local landscape. The same threshold was applied to the number of established home sales in the neighbourhood.

There were 14 areas that met these criteria, covering the breadth of England from Essex to Yorkshire to Northumberland.

In 10 of those areas, house price growth was weaker near the new-build homes compared with the rest of the area. In nine of those 10 areas, homes near new estates lost value.

Skelmanthorpe, six miles outside the Peak District National Park in West Yorkshire, is the most extreme example of the trend.

In 2024, Land Registry transaction records indicated a cluster of 53 new-build sales within a quarter-mile radius. On a former patch of grassland on the town’s west side, the new suburb of Weavers Place had sprouted.

In the two years before this, existing detached and semi-detached homes within half a mile of the new development sold for an average of £361,000. In the two years after, this had plunged 18.5pc to £295,000.

Slightly further out in Skelmanthorpe – between half and three quarters of a mile from the site, and so with access to all the same services, bus connections and green space – prices rose by 5.9pc over the same period.

Nigel Bishop, of buying agency Recoco Property Search, said: “The majority of buyers wanting to purchase a home in the countryside seek quiet and natural surroundings.

“A new-built complex in eyeshot of their desired property would very much disturb the peace and aesthetics associated with country living, making the property less desirable to house hunters.”

He also explained that potential buyers could be concerned about additional developments being built in the future.

He said: “House hunters would then also be concerned about the area’s general infrastructure and how such developments impact the existing drainage system, Wi-Fi signal and whether the roads can accommodate increased traffic.

“The fear of the unknown and what is yet to come will cause buyers to walk away.”

Skelmanthorpe may be the most extreme example from the dataset, but it is by no means the only countryside location where a new development hurt local house prices.

In 2021, construction began on a new development of 254 homes called Bingham Gate in the market town of Bingham, Nottinghamshire. Since the project was completed, homes within half a mile of Bingham Gate have decreased in value by 10.1pc, from £379,058 to £340,787. Meanwhile, properties further away increased in value by 0.7pc to £362,525.

After Yew Tree Park came to Burscough, West Lancs, house prices nearby dropped by 3.2pc, whereas homes more than half a mile away increased in value by 4.5pc.

It is a similar story in Bourne Springs, a development in South Kesteven, Lincs, where local house prices fell by 6.9pc, and Whittlesey Park in Fenland, Cambs, where existing property values dropped by 4.1pc. Prices also fell in the wider area but not by as much.

Labour has pledged to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament yet the Government has so far failed to meet its yearly target of 300,000.

In fact, housebuilding has continued to decline in the worst slowdown since the pandemic. Rating agency S&P Global’s housebuilding subindex fell to 33.5 in December, the lowest reading since May 2020.

Residential applications for planning permission fell 9pc during Labour’s first year in office, while completions dropped almost 13pc to their lowest 12-month tally in nearly a decade.

But the Government is introducing measures designed to accelerate housebuilding. In November the Ministry of Housing announced that housebuilding near well-connected railway stations will be given the green light by default.

Former housing secretary Angela Rayner’s hallmark Planning and Infrastructure Bill finally became law in December. In bad news for rural homeowners and their house prices, her successor Steve Reed has said this means property developers will be “getting spades in the ground faster” from now on.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Overworked staff to blame for ghost train fiasco, says regulator
data.description:
Overworked staff were to blame for a timetabling blunder that left empty “ghost trains” running from Manchester to London, the head of Britain’s rail regulator said.

John Larkinson, the chief executive of the Office of Rail and Road (ORR), told MPs that the department was too “stretched” by its workload to consider every application to run trains.

This led the ORR to refuse Avanti West Coast permission to operate a 7am passenger service from Manchester Piccadilly to London Euston – even though the train had to leave at that time to be available for a 9.30am departure from the capital to Glasgow.

The decision meant that a fully crewed, 600-seat Pendolino train stood at Piccadilly with its doors locked before heading south in full view of passengers, who were left to wait before cramming on to later services.

Uproar over the “ghost trains” led Peter Hendy, the rail minister, and Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, to intervene to force Mr Larkinson to reinstate the 7am service – with Ms Alexander saying that “passengers were rightly baffled” by the ORR’s original decision.

However, in a letter to the Commons transport select committee, Mr Larkinson pointed to a clash between the ORR and Network Rail as an underlying cause of the fiasco.

Growing tensions

ORR staff had inherited a huge workload of timetable changes dumped on it by the track operator, which refused to carry out its usual role of allocating train routes, he claimed.

Since the ORR lacked the specialist resources of the far larger Network Rail, he wrote, “the whole process was running late,” leaving the regulator – which would normally act only as a final arbiter – “stretched and trying to close out multiple interacting decisions”.

He told MPs: “Network Rail’s approach to recent applications has been to decline to agree any new access contracts, even if it supports them.

“As a consequence we were working through 82 complex and competing track access applications relating to the December 2025 timetable, within constrained timescales.

“Acting as the first-instance decision-maker, rather than as a reviewer, placed greater demands than usual on us.”

The comments hint at growing tensions within the railway industry, as different bodies jostle for position before nationalisation.

Network Rail will be merged with train operators to create the new Great British Railways, while the ORR is expected to see its powers diminished, occupying more of an advisory role.

Mr Larkinson, who is leaving the ORR after 20 years in April, said the ORR had initially chosen to block the 7am train to create a “firebreak” between services that would help cope with any delays to crucial morning services.

He admitted that the decision was a mistake but said staff had understood that the empty train would run to London from its depot without a crew and fitting in between other services.

While Avanti later notified the ORR that the train would in fact run empty from Piccadilly, this was not “escalated appropriately”, he said.

A spokesman for Network Rail said it provided detailed recommendations on all 82 route applications that were before the ORR, including the Manchester departure, which it had proposed should be approved

data.title: Billionaire former Tory donor pays himself £477m
data.description:
A billionaire former Tory donor paid himself £477m last year as revenues soared at his City hedge fund.

Chris Rokos cemented his position as one of Britain’s richest men, and is believed to be the highest-paid partner at his eponymous fund, Rokos Capital Management, which more than doubled its turnover from £445m to £1.2bn.

The fund’s latest accounts revealed that the total pool of profits for its 23 partners rose to a record £940m in the 12 months to March, up from £315m the year prior.

While the results do not disclose the identity of the highest-paid partner, Mr Rokos is widely considered the fund’s top earner, who founded the firm in 2015 and is its majority owner.

Mr Rokos, who is one of Britain’s leading hedge fund managers, was also previously a donor to the Conservatives, giving the party around £2m between 2009 and 2015. He made his last donation in 2018.

He also owns the centuries-old, Grade I listed Tottenham House, one of England’s grandest stately homes. Earlier this year, Mr Rokos made headlines by applying for planning permission to install around 1,100 solar panels to provide power to the 100-bed mansion.

Under the plans, two acres of the manor estate – currently used for silage and grazing dairy cows – would be developed to house solar panels. These would provide nearly one third of the power required to run the estate.

Tottenham House was part of the Savernake Estate, once a royal hunting forest with significant links to Henry VIII. The family of Jane Seymour, the King’s third wife, took ownership of the home before passing it down through several generations.

Mr Rokos has been working to restore the house into a family home.

He has launched a series of ambitious construction projects, such as underground tunnels connecting the main house to the stables. He has also installed a tennis pavilion and a cinema.

Before launching Rokos Capital, he also co-founded Brevan Howard, another City hedge fund.

Unlike most large hedge funds, which have multiple portfolio managers making bets on the market, Mr Rokos is the main trader at Rokos Capital.

He is supported by a team of analysts and senior investment professionals, which the firm calls investment officers.

Rokos Capital Management was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Burnham’s skyscraper tycoon takes £40m dividend after taxpayer loan
data.description:
A skyscraper tycoon backed by Andy Burnham took £40m in dividends from a taxpayer-backed project in Manchester, The Telegraph can reveal.

Daren Whitaker moved the money over a period of three years from WB Developments, a company backed by Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA).

It was received by another company owned by Mr Whitaker.

The disclosures, contained in Companies House filings, will heighten scrutiny of the relationship between the GMCA and Mr Whitaker, who has received more than £700m in taxpayer loans to build luxury skyscrapers in Manchester.

Under a loan agreement seen by The Telegraph, Mr Whitaker’s WB Developments was barred from paying dividends “except with the prior written consent of the lender”.

The dividends were awarded in instalments between 2017 and 2019 from WB Developments, which received £42.5m in cash from the GMCA to help build Wilburn Basin, a 491-bedroom apartment block in Salford.

The first was a £17.7m dividend in 2017, followed by £15.4m and £6.8m in payments in 2018 and 2019.

Mr Whitaker is the ultimate owner of WB Developments, according to filings on Companies House, which falls within his Renaker empire.

The payments were made despite restrictions on dividend payments contained in the initial £42.5m loan agreement between the GMCA, which is chaired by the Mayor of Greater Manchester, and Mr Whitaker for Wilburn Basin. It was struck in 2016 before Mr Burnham was elected Mayor of Greater Manchester.

Asked about the dividends last night, the GMCA refused to say whether officials were aware of the payments or if they granted approval.

The loan was to be repaid in July 2018, two years after construction started on Wilburn Basin.

A spokesman for the GMCA said: “The loan in question was repaid in full, with interest, eight years ago. It was repaid nine months before the deadline, bringing significant benefits to the taxpayer.”

The loans from the GMCA to Mr Whitaker have prompted a High Court claim from rival developer Aubrey Weis. He has alleged Renaker was able to cash in on Manchester’s building boom by unfairly accessing public funds.

Mr Weis has argued that the loans were effectively subsidies for Renaker, which used the cash to build tower blocks that contained no affordable housing. Renaker has not commented and the GMCA has consistently denied the allegations.

A ruling handed down by the Competition Appeal Tribunal last year rejected Mr Weis’s claims.

He is appealing against the decision.

Mr Burnham has sought to defend the loans, telling the BBC last month that the GMCA supported Renaker because “London-based lenders wouldn’t give us the money”.

“They had this downer on Manchester,” he said.

The Telegraph revealed late last year that Mr Whitaker had listed his main residence as Monaco on Companies House. However, this was later changed back to Britain.

When asked about Mr Whitaker’s residency last month, Mr Burnham denied knowledge of his circumstances but said: “I can’t comment on people’s decisions about their lives. People have got to be free to where they live.

“It’s not the case that someone has walked away with anything other than having invested in our city and built it out. The towers are helping us to bring investors to the city.”

After building a string of different skyscrapers, Renaker is now pursuing its most ambitious project to date.

This is called the Lighthouse, a skyscraper consisting of 642 high-end flats with a restaurant on its top floor.

Renaker was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘I was quoted £4k a year home insurance for my chocolate box thatch cottage’
data.description:
When public sector sales director Russell Kirby, 63, and his wife, Sarah, first saw their thatched cottage in the village of Dunkeswell in East Devon, they couldn’t contain their excitement.

They had been living in a detached 1950s house in Lindford, Hampshire, where Russell hadn’t really felt at home.

All that changed when they saw the four-bed chocolate box cottage with roses around the door and a large garden, perfect for their four dogs.

After putting in their best offer of £530,000, the pair were overjoyed when it was accepted in summer 2023.

However, one thing they hadn’t factored in was the enormous cost of building insurance, which for their new Grade II-listed home was quoted – at one point – at just shy of £4,000 a year.

A survey completed in their second year of ownership in 2025 put the home’s rebuild cost (how much it would cost to reinstate if destroyed) at just over £1m, which sent their building insurance spiralling.

The same thing is happening to other thatched property owners, with some saying they feel they are being held to ransom by insurers. One owner in Devon said their premium for a three-bed semi-detached Grade II-listed home had almost tripled, from £470 in 2018 to £1,300 this year.

Thatched homes tend to have higher insurance premiums, as fire damage is often severe and rebuilding is a long, specialised process.

But homeowners have told The Telegraph that they are now concerned that traditional thatched cottages will disappear altogether as people choose to tile non-listed properties, rather than re-thatch them, to make them easier to sell.

“My wife really wanted the house; both of us fell in love with it,” says. “I’d holidayed nearby as a child, so there were a lot of happy memories linked to the area.

“This home is my children’s inheritance, but because of these sky-high insurance costs, I do worry that some of these houses could become impossible to sell.”

The Kirbys had to get building insurance to exchange contracts on the house.

It was a probate sale, and the previous owner had insured through the National Farmers Union (NFU) Mutual, which was also the couple’s insurer at the time.

Their old home had cost them about £600 a year for both buildings and contents insurance.

Russell assumed that switching to his new home would incur an increase; it was a Grade II-listed building, after all. But he was not prepared for what that increase would be.

As the exchange loomed, Russell approached the NFU Mutual for a quote and was asked about the rebuild value. He did not know, so he just told them the home’s value of £530,000.

His quote came out at £2,808 a year, and the couple was so close to completion that they had no choice but to take the NFU Mutual up on it.

There were also strict conditions: no open flames within 100m of the house, so no barbecues, garden fires or patio heaters.

Their boiler and Aga cooker had to be serviced annually, and both chimneys in use had to be swept every 12 months, adding a further cost of around £500 a year on top of insurance.

Then, two years into living in their dream home, an NFU surveyor visited the house to audit the fire extinguishers, another condition of the policy.

“They asked again about the rebuild cost, and we had to have a survey,” Russell says. “It turned out we had been massively underinsured, and the cost of rebuilding the house was actually £1,000,224.”

New calculations then saw his premiums leap to £3,998 a year.

Having the wrong rebuild estimate could put millions of other homeowners in peril.

According to Rebuild Cost Assessment, as of September 2025, only 7pc of UK properties are correctly insured, with the average underinsured home covered for just 67pc of its actual rebuild cost.

That means that if your home is completely destroyed, you may not receive enough to rebuild it, or you may receive nothing at all.

A landscape of rising costs

According to the Association of British Insurers (ABI), the annual average price of combined building and contents insurance in the second quarter of 2025 was £391.

This rises to an average of £648 for a Grade I property and £555 for a Grade II building, according to the latest available Confused.com data from the last quarter of 2024.

Those figures may already be out of date – experts say rebuild costs have shot up massively since Brexit, Covid and the war in Ukraine.

An ABI spokesperson said: “Listed buildings or properties with unique constructions often carry additional and specific risks that require tailored insurance. For example, thatched cottages can present a higher fire risk.”

According to broker Howden, post-pandemic material cost rises have now stabilised, but new environmental legislation and climbing labour costs are pushing rebuild prices up.

The construction workforce is down by over 365,000 workers compared to 2008, and a third of skilled tradespeople – particularly relevant to listed buildings – are set to retire within the next decade.

Russell says the monthly payments were not crippling to him and his wife, Sarah, 56, a personal assistant, while they were working, but the cost would have been unaffordable after retirement.

Russell eventually shopped around and secured a much more affordable quote of £1,494 a year for both buildings and contents.

Tom Chapman, of The Home Insurer, a non-standard broker that now insures the Kirbys via underwriter Aviva, says owners should consider increasing their excess, removing wood-burning stoves, increasing their chimney height and ensuring their contents are not over-insured to bring down premiums.

An NFU Mutual spokesman said: “Thatched homes present unique risks and are often made of non-standard materials, which, if needed, will require specialist repairs, which can be significantly more expensive than the standard.”

They said his insurance increased by less than 7pc between 2023 and 2025, and the latest renewal saw a larger rise, since his sums insured increased by over 80pc.

data.title: Give under-22s free bus travel, says Green Party
data.description:
The Green Party is calling for free bus travel in England for those under the age of 22.

Zack Polanski, the party’s leader, said introducing the universal bus pass for the group could help young people shut out of employment and training by the cost of living.

The party said the scheme would be self-financing, arguing that increased bus usage would boost the economy.

Mr Polanski said: “Young people are increasingly shut out of employment and training by the cost of living crisis, which often hits the youngest the hardest.

“This Labour Government has made a dire situation for young people even worse with the poorest getting even poorer and rich getting even richer.

“Free bus travel for the young is one part of improving the lives of younger people and showing what a richer and greener country looks like if we invest in people and not hand over our assets to the super-rich and corporations.”

The scheme would bring England into line with Scotland, where those aged five to 22 have been entitled to free bus travel since 2022.

Under-18s in London already travel for free on buses with other discounts available for some up to the age of 25, while some bus companies across England also offer discounts for young people.

Mr Polanski said Green Party councillors, if elected in May’s local polls, will seek to bring in the universal bus scheme in their areas.

He said: “Green councillors elected in May will be using their influence to implement this scheme in their areas and we will be applying political pressure at a national level for a scheme recommended by the transport select committee last year to be made real.

“Free bus travel for the young is part of our mission to make hope normal again.”

The party pointed to research by KPMG estimating that 2.2 million commuters rely on buses to get to work and generate £72bn in collective income each year.

Anyone at state pension age in Britain can currently claim a national older person’s bus pass.

In London those over 60 can get an Oyster card with free travel around the capital.

data.title: Trump warned credit card crackdown will trigger financial crisis for millions
data.description:
Donald Trump’s crackdown on credit cards risks pushing millions of poor Americans into financial crisis, some of Wall Street’s most powerful groups have warned.

Mr Trump is pressing ahead with plans to temporarily cap credit card interest rates for 12 months at 10pc from January 20.

However, the proposal is facing a backlash over fears it will force banks to cancel cards and pull back lending to consumers with the lowest credit scores.

On Monday, the American Bankers Association (ABA), which represents major lenders like JP Morgan, hedge fund billionaire Bill Ackman and the Bank Policy Institute (BPI) all rounded on Mr Trump over the plan.

Mr Ackman, who has publicly supported Mr Trump, added: “My concern about capping rates at 10pc is that doing so will inevitably cause millions of Americans to have their cards cancelled as credit card companies lose the ability to adequately price subprime credit risk.

“Consumers denied credit cards will be forced to turn to loan sharks whose rates and terms will be vastly worse for borrowers.”

The BPI said the cap would “be devastating for millions of American families and small business owners … the very consumers this proposal intends to help”. The ABA said the move would push consumers towards “less regulated, more costly alternatives”.

Mr Trump’s announcement sent shares in US credit card providers sliding on Monday, with Capital One leading the fallers plunging by as much as 6.24pc.

In the UK, as much as £8bn was wiped off the market value of British banks. Shares in Barclays, which has a US credit card business, fell by 3.3pc in London. Shares in NatWest were also down by 1.2pc, even though the bank does not operate a US credit card business.

The plan has stoked widespread fears because credit cards are so vital for the US economy, with the number of accounts hitting a record 642 million in the third quarter of 2025.

This means the US has nearly twice as many credit card accounts as it does people. Total credit card debt is at a record $1.23tn (£891bn) – up by 39pc since the pandemic began.

In theory, capping at 10pc should dramatically reduce borrowers’ costs because it would more than halve the average rate paid, which was 22.5pc in November.

At no point since at least 1994, when Federal Reserve data began, has the rate been as low as 10pc. According to Vanderbilt University, a 10pc cap would save US consumers $73bn net a year.

However, analysts warn it is precisely the people who Trump is trying to help that will be hit the hardest by his rate cap because it will force banks to do less lending.

Justin Begley, economist at Moody’s Analytics said: “If the government does indeed enforce this policy on the banks, then it’s just going to become completely unprofitable to lend to people that are of higher credit risk.

“I would disagree with people who are saying that the banks can absorb this. The banks have to be able to properly price credit risk.”

Banks may simply cancel subprime borrowers’ credit cards outright, Mr Begley added. “That’s something I have heard banks talking about doing. They’re going to want to protect themselves.”

Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar said: “It is likely that lower rates will push credit providers to reassess their lending criteria and the policy ends up creating more harm than good.”

Subprime borrowers have a credit card default rate of 1.2pc, double the overall default rate, according to Moody’s. Currently, subprime borrowers make up 21pc of accounts in default. During the financial crisis, the rate was closer to half.

The measure will hit at a time when low and middle income US households are becoming increasingly overwhelmed with their debts.

The share of Americans who are falling seriously behind on their credit card payments has already soared to a near 15-year high.

Debt advice firms have been warning that America’s cost of living crisis is increasingly hitting middle class households, as families turn to credit just to cover the costs of basic necessities.

data.title: ​Oliver Marriott, author of The Property Boom who restored the Yorkshire manor house Grimston Garth
data.description:
​Oliver Marriott​, who has died aged 86, was a financial journalist and property company director who restored an exceptional East Yorkshire mansion.

In his early career, Marriott was property editor of Investors’ Chronicle, financial editor of The Times and the author of well-praised investigative books, The Property Boom (1967) and Anatomy of a Merger (1970).

The former shone light on the machinations of new-rich tycoons in a fast-moving market, ruffling feathers and attracting several legal threats. The latter (co-authored with Robert Jones) explored the amalgamation of the ambitious General Electric Co led by Arnold Weinstock with the more traditional Associated Electrical Industries and English Electric to form a British industrial titan of its era.

In 1969, Marriott left journalism to become a property player in his own right. He joined forces with Jeffrey (later Lord) Sterling and Bruce McPhail in Sterling Guarantee Trust, acquired from the Slater Walker investment group, which they rapidly expanded by acquiring lame-duck companies with attractive assets – Marriott bringing depth of knowledge as the in-house m​arket analyst.

The 1970 redevelopment of the Gamages department store site in Oxford Street was an early success.

The shrewd Sterling operation survived the crash of 1974 to emerge, with Bank of England and lenders’ support, as the rescuer of the much larger Town & City group – the sector’s biggest potential casualty as owner of Arndale shopping centres and other commercial properties valued at their peak at £600 million. Later, Sterling Guarantee merged with the shipping and property giant P&O, where Marriott was a director until 1991.

Meanwhile, in 1972 Marriott had bought Grimston Garth, a late-18th-century Gothic manor house designed by John Carr of York on a triangular footprint around a central hexagon, with castellated corner towers, in the Holderness district of East Yorkshire. Private, modest and independent in spirit, Marriott delighted in the restoration and care of the Grimston estate for the rest of his life.

Oliver Digby James Marriott was born in West Sussex on August 27 1939, one of four children of Rowland Marriott and his wife Evelyn, née Caillard.

Descended from a line of learned Leicestershire clergymen (though there were also ancestral connections in Yorkshire), Oliver was educated at Eton but shunned the family tradition of going on to study at Oxford. Instead, he became a clerk in an aggregates company in Pimlico and later with Rothschilds in the City.

In 1961 he was victorious as a Labour candidate for Lutterworth Rural District Council in Leicestershire, becoming the youngest councillor in the country. But he was bored with his banking job – and in the same year he discovered his true métier when he joined Investors’ Chronicle under the wing of its pioneering property editors Charles Gordon and Gary Arnott.

After a stint on The Statist magazine, he moved in 1965 to The Sunday Times (where the then deputy editor William Rees-Mogg gave him a month off to finish the property book) and was financial editor of The Times from 1967 to 1969.

In his later career, Marriott was founder chairman of two smaller property companies, Churchbury Estates and Ilex, and a non-executive director of Shaftesbury, which owned tracts of Soho and Covent Garden.

A committed Eurosceptic, he stood in the 2005 general election as the Ukip candidate for the Conservative-held Beverley and Holderness constituency, attracting just 4.7 per cent of the poll with 2,336 votes.

His older brother Richard, a City financier who was Lord-Lieutenant of East Yorkshire, restored another historic house, Boynton Hall, a few miles north of Grimston; the brothers shared a particular love of books.

Passionate in his devotion to family life, Oliver Marriott married, in 1964, Lif Kristina Rolandsdottir from Stockholm, who survives him with their two daughters.

​Oliver Marriott, born August 27 1939, died December 5 2025

data.title: Elon Musk has parked his tanks on BT’s lawn
data.description:
As the repressive regime in Iran closed down the internet, it came to the rescue. It has become a vital tool for Ukrainians in their battle against the Russians. And more mundanely, it allows you to send a couple of WhatsApps while you are flying across the Atlantic, if you’re on the right airline.

We typically think of Elon Musk’s satellite-based broadband provider Starlink as only being useful in a handful of limited circumstances. But that is starting to change. Musk is about to park his formidable tanks on the lawns of BT and the rest of Britain’s internet companies – and not before time, the whole market could easily be blown apart.

After a dreadful few years, 2025 was a lot better for Britain’s largest telecoms business, BT. The shares rose by almost 30pc over the last year, and the threat of a takeover attempt receded as its performance improved. Its huge capital spend on upgrading its broadband network is nearing completion, allowing it to generate lots of easy profits, while smaller rivals were starting to go under. Only last week, the “vulture fund” FitzWalter Capital applied to put G Network, which provides high-speed broadband in London, into administration.

Allison Kirkby, BT’s chief executive, is probably hoping for a decent year. But broadband incumbents like BT are now facing a new threat that none of them saw coming: Musk.

Sure, he has plenty of critics, and the Government led by Sir Keir Starmer is shamefully trying to hustle Musk out of the country. But there is no denying that Musk is the greatest entrepreneur of his generation. As well as building Tesla into a major force in cars and transforming the social media site X, he has turned SpaceX and its Starlink unit into a company now worth $800bn (£600bn).

One reason why investors are putting such a huge value on the company is becoming clear. It is becoming a viable domestic broadband provider. In the UK, Starlink has just started offering high-speed internet for as little as £35 a month, down from £55, in certain areas. That is cheaper than BT’s equivalent £40 service and the £36-a-month Virgin Media offer. Even once the connection fee is taken into account, it still works out cheaper than BT over a two-year contract. It is starting to become a player.

The mainstream view in the telecoms industry is that satellite broadband will never be a mass market proposition. “In fixed broadband, satellite costs are still a multiple of the average costs of traditional networks, only becoming competitive for a proportion of the hardest 1pc of households to reach,” argued a note from Enders Analysis. Well, perhaps. For now, it is certainly true that providing broadband access via satellite is much more expensive than via a fibre-optic cable running along your street. It will struggle to attract more than a handful of customers living in very remote areas.

But that is not necessarily the whole story. Musk has deep, deep pockets. SpaceX is already worth more than 40 times BT’s entire market value, and once it completes an expected Initial Public Offering later this year, Musk is likely to become the world’s first trillionaire. He has the money, and we know from his record that he doesn’t mind spending it if he needs to build a business. Nor does he care very much about what his shareholders think. If he has to make some losses for a few years to build a position in the British, or indeed the wider European broadband industry, he will be perfectly willing to do so. It will pay off soon enough, and even if it doesn’t, the losses will hardly break him.

SpaceX can raise vast sums of fresh capital whenever it wants, at virtually zero cost. If he needs money to expand, it will be available. That certainly is not true of BT or any of the other incumbents. One point is surely clear. Musk is the most restlessly hyper-competitive tycoon on the planet, and he builds businesses mainly for the satisfaction of it. If he sees an opportunity, he will seize it and bulldoze aside any obstacles.

For consumers, of course, that is very welcome. The broadband market has been characterised by high prices and often very poor service. An analysis by Uswitch, published only this week, found that millions of households face an 11pc rise in broadband prices this year, or more than three times the rate of inflation, as fixed-price deals come to an end. Meanwhile, anyone who has been stuck on the phone for hours talking to what is optimistically called a “helpline” – probably an AI chatbot – won’t need much persuading to switch to a cheaper offering, especially if it has a faster or more reliable service.

To put this politely, broadband is not a business with a great deal of brand loyalty. All the major players have focused on cutting costs and ramping up profits rather than keeping their customers happy. That works fine so long as there isn’t much competition. When a rival appears, however, it can quickly prove very costly.

Right now, the major broadband players view Starlink as a peripheral provider serving a small niche market. So, probably, do most customers. But the auto giants thought that about Tesla, and we all know how that worked out for them. In reality, Musk can build a whole new market in less time than it takes traditional businesses such as BT to organise a “strategy awayday”. They underestimate him at their peril. Starlink is parking its tank, or rather its satellites or their lawns. BT needs to come up with a response and fast – no one should bet against Musk blowing the competition away.

data.title: Morrisons’ losses hit £381m after steep debt costs
data.description:
Morrisons plunged to a £381m loss last year as it battled steep borrowing costs and weaker consumer spending.

The private equity-owned grocer revealed that its debt interest bill hit £281m in 2025, derailing attempts to turn a profit.

Despite ending the year once again in the red, bosses hailed a drop in net debt to £3.1bn, down from £3.5bn last summer.

The retailer’s initial results announcement also did not include any mention of the £381m loss. Joanna Goff, the chief financial officer, only revealed the figure when pressed during a subsequent media call on Wednesday.

She said this was a £33m improvement on last year, while also pointing to £835m of so-called adjusted earnings, which strip out interest costs and tax.

Morrisons has been battling elevated debt costs since its £10bn takeover by private equity firm Clayton, Dubilier & Rice (CD&R) in 2021.

It has been working to reduce its debt bill by selling off some of its supermarkets before leasing them back.

The latest figures covered what Rami Baitiéh, Morrisons’ chief executive, described as a “challenging period”.

He said: “We saw the impact of the government cost increases, with inflation and Budget uncertainty all weighing on customer sentiment.”

Mr Baitiéh said the overall grocery market had been in decline towards the end of its financial year, which ends in October.

Morrisons’s like-for-like sales fell by 2.4pc in the three months to the end of October but were up 2.8pc over the year as a whole. Yearly revenues were up 3.2pc to £15.8bn.

Mr Baitiéh said Morrisons also had more pensioners and less wealthy shoppers in its stores, meaning they were more sensitive to price pressures.

Mr Baitiéh urged the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs to help keep prices down.

“I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up,” he said.

The figures will fuel speculation that Morrisons could soon be set to lose its position as Britain’s fifth biggest supermarket.

Lidl has been gaining ground on Morrisons in recent months, with Worldpanel figures suggesting it held 8.1pc of the grocery market compared to Morrisons’ 8.3pc in November.

Mr Baitiéh argued that the Worldpanel figures did not include its growth in convenience stores, where it has been expanding in recent months.

He said Morrisons was “happy with our like-for-like” figures in larger supermarkets, adding: “When we look at Morrisons, we are addressing the price, we are addressing the promotions, we are addressing the loyalty, we are addressing the availability.”

data.title: Trump sues New York Times over negative opinion poll
data.description:
Donald Trump announced that he will sue The New York Times for publishing a negative opinion poll about his leadership.

“The Times Siena poll... will be added to my lawsuit against the failing New York Times,” Mr Trump said on his Truth Social platform. “They will be held fully responsible for all of their radical Left lies and wrongdoing!”

Mr Trump reacted after the publication of a New York Times/Siena University poll showing his approval rating at just 40 per cent, a figure which is in line with multiple other surveys pointing to declining support a year into his second term.

The US president also suggested that what he called “fake” surveys should be criminalised.

“Fake and fraudulent polling should be, virtually, a criminal offence,” he wrote.

Trump sues multiple media companies

Mr Trump has launched a string of defamation lawsuits against media companies, including the BBC, CNN, Wall Street Journal, CBS and ABC, with some ending in multi-million-dollar settlements.

He first sued The New York Times for $15bn (£11.1bn) in September 2025 for defamation, alleging the paper published false reports to damage his 2024 presidential campaign and reputation.

A federal judge dismissed the case, but it was refiled in amended form a month later.

The Times responded by calling that new lawsuit “an attempt to stifle independent reporting” and “intimidation”.

The Times/Siena poll is widely regarded as one of the most accurate and influential of US political surveys. Its latest findings were notable for what the paper described as the unravelling of Trump’s winning 2024 coalition.

It found that less than a third of voters think the US is better off than it was when Mr Trump returned to the White House a year ago, with a wide majority saying he has focused on the wrong issues.

It also found that a majority of voters disapprove of how Mr Trump has handled top issues including the economy, immigration, the war between Russia and Ukraine and his actions in Venezuela. And significantly, a majority of Americans, 51 per cent, said that Mr Trump’s policies had made life less affordable for them.

Only 42 per cent of voters rated Mr Trump’s first year as a success.

Trump has previously targeted pollsters. In December 2024, he sued Iowa pollster Ann Selzer, accusing her of consumer fraud and “brazen election interference” after she published a poll showing Kamala Harris leading him by three points in Iowa. He dropped the federal lawsuit in June 2025.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Turbines waste enough wind power to supply all of London’s homes
data.description:
Britain’s wind farm turbines wasted enough energy to power all of London’s homes last year, new figures show.

A record 10 terawatt hours (TWh) of wind power went to waste in 2025, according to a report from energy analyst Montel – costing billpayers a total of £1.4bn in “curtailment costs”.

This was up 22pc on the year before, as growing strain on the grid prevented wind power from being transported to the cities and towns that need it most.

This results in so-called curtailment costs, which are paid to wind farms when they are asked to switch off.

At the same time, grid operators must call on gas plants to step in and keep the lights on with replacement power, often at great expense.

Analysts said that wind farms in Scotland were largely to blame.

Montel’s report said: “The amount of renewable electricity curtailed in Great Britain in 2025 (10TWh) could have met the combined electricity demand of every domestic household in London for the entire year.

“Northern Scotland has seen the most curtailment by far. Over 8.8TWh of wind power in northern Scotland was switched off, enough to also power all Scottish domestic electricity demand for the year.”

The cost of curtailment has been growing as Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, rolls out more renewable energy sources – such as wind and solar.

As well as paying wind developers, Montel said solar farms are also being asked to switch off because of bottlenecks.

Montel said: “Solar curtailment costs rose over the year to total over £252,000. While this is substantially lower than the corresponding figures for wind, it represents a rise from the negligible costs associated with solar curtailment in 2024.”

To combat curtailment, regulator Ofgem has approved plans for the UK’s three transmission operators – National Grid, Scottish Power and SSE – to spend up to £90bn on new lines and substations.

However, these will take at least five years to build, with constraint payments set to continue rising.

Octopus Energy has warned that such payments could see £8bn added to bills by 2030.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, has blamed Mr Miliband’s planned expansion of renewables for the recent uptick in costs.

The Energy Secretary has approved large numbers of new wind and solar projects as part of his plan to decarbonise the grid by 2030.

She said: “We are paying more than ever before to pay wind farms to switch off when the wind blows.

“Costs are set to triple by 2030 as he approves more wind farms than ever before. He cares far more about his own Clean Power 2030 target than looking after consumers.”

A spokesman for SSE said: “ The UK has built renewables where the resources are strongest, and now we need to upgrade the grid to eliminate bottlenecks so we can harness more of that energy, more of the time.”

The National Energy System Operator said it was working to keep energy costs down but stressed that grid expansion was crucial.

A spokesman said: “Without the crucial expansion of the electricity network by those who own it, and industry working together to deliver the changes needed, there is a risk that curtailment costs will continue to rise.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Trump may be winning his war on the media
data.description:
When Tony Dokoupil, the host of CBS Evening News, secured his first interview with Donald Trump this week, he was probably hoping for a scoop.

Instead, the journalist found himself subject to ridicule over claims he had only been appointed for coverage seen as sympathetic.

“If [Kamala Harris] got in, you probably wouldn’t have a job right now,” said Trump.

The comments appeared to be acknowledge what many in liberal US media circles had long feared: a bow to Trump at CBS after the $8bn (£5.9bn) takeover of its parent company, Paramount, by billionaire Trump ally Larry Ellison.

Dokoupil had already drawn criticism for a segment in which he flattered Marco Rubio, the US secretary of state, and for his reporting of the anniversary of the Jan 6 Capitol riots, which was branded a “both-sides mess”.

Few would dispute that CBS has undergone a significant ideological shift in recent months.

Bari Weiss, an outspoken “anti-woke” journalist, was appointed CBS News’s editor-in-chief in October. She rose to prominence as a star columnist bemoaning the rise of “woke” culture and what she saw as the growing intolerance of American liberals.

When she took the last-minute decision to pull a segment from CBS’s flagship news show, 60 Minutes, about a notorious El Salvadoran prison to which Trump has been deporting migrants, there were cries of censorship.

Weiss defended the move, arguing that the piece was not ready and insisting CBS’s coverage was “comprehensive and fair”.

But CBS’s shift demonstrates the dramatic effect that the president’s media war is having on the news landscape. A year into his second term, there are signs his strategy of bending journalists to his will is paying off.

The New York Times has long been seen as a bastion of American journalistic integrity, promising “all the news that’s fit to print” for more than a century. But even America’s most-esteemed media organisation is seemingly not above playing Trump’s game.

Readers this month accused the newspaper of kowtowing for access to the president after four of its journalists carried out a two-hour interview with him, which was subsequently published in full.

Reporters were accused of pulling punches to curry favour with the administration. “They are just Fox News attached to a crossword company,” wrote one disenchanted reader on Reddit.

How has Trump done it?

According to strategists, the president has adopted a dual-pronged approach that has seen him muzzle critical outlets in the mainstream media while also overwhelming the news cycle with a barrage of inflammatory comments and initiatives.

It helps that Trump, the former Apprentice host who has made cameos in films including Home Alone and Zoolander, is undoubtedly the most media-savvy president in US history.

More so than any predecessor, the 79-year-old former reality TV star has craved attention and basked in the limelight.

Yet intimidating media outlets – including the BBC – through litigation is one of his main tactics in an attempt to bend editors to his will.

He recently sued the BBC for $10bn over its misleading editing of his speech in a Panorama documentary, which was first uncovered by The Telegraph.

Sinead O’Callaghan, a managing partner at Cooke, Young & Keidan, said the claim was “as much a tactic to exert pressure on the BBC as it is a statement of legal intent”.

The BBC, for its part, has filed a motion to dismiss Trump’s lawsuit, arguing that Florida is an “improper” court venue, that contrary to his claims, the documentary did not air in the US and that Trump has failed to show it caused him harm.

Regardless of the outcome, however, the legal battle piles further pressure on the beleaguered public service broadcaster while diverting time and resources just as it enters crucial talks over the future of the licence fee.

Even before taking office for the second time, Trump was throwing his weight around through the courts. He successfully sued Disney-owned ABC for defamation after anchor George Stephanopoulos falsely said the president had been found “liable for rape”.

CBS News then agreed to a $16m settlement after Trump accused the network of “deceitful” editing of a 60 Minutes interview with Kamala Harris.

Trump has also filed mammoth defamation lawsuits against both The New York Times and Rupert Murdoch’s Wall Street Journal.

But he has also sought to undermine mainstream media outlets in other ways, too.

Last year, the president slashed federal funding to public broadcasters NPR and PBS under an executive order entitled: “Ending Taxpayer Subsidization of Biased Media”.

He has banned Associated Press journalists from much of the White House, while a host of major publications have relinquished access to the Pentagon after Pete Hegseth, the defence secretary, introduced stringent new reporting restrictions.

While shutting out mainstream media outlets, Trump has invited Right-wing podcasters and YouTubers, including the controversial commentator Jack Posobiec, to briefings.

At times, Trump’s assault on the media has appeared personal, especially when women are involved. In November, he called a female New York Times journalist “ugly” and said “quiet piggy” to a Bloomberg reporter.

In a concerning escalation, the FBI this week raided the home of a Washington Post journalist who was investigating the sacking of government workers. The Post branded the raid, ostensibly part of a crackdown on government leaks, “highly unusual and aggressive”.

Yet Trump’s antagonism towards traditional media has not stopped him from seeking the spotlight – the second prong to his media blitz.

It is a technique known as “flooding the zone”, a term attributed to Trump’s former strategist Steve Bannon. Bannon told journalist Michael Lewis during Trump’s first term: “The real opposition is the media. And the way to deal with them is to flood the zone with s--- .”

The president weighs in incessantly on all manner of issues – from major geopolitical incidents to a row over a jeans advert starring actress Sydney Sweeney – in an attempt to spark controversy and dominate the agenda.

“We joke internally that he is our ultimate director of communications,” Karoline Leavitt, Trump’s press secretary, has said.

She added: “He has incredible media instincts, and he is the final decision maker on all policy, and he has been in a ‘flood the zone’, ‘do as much as possible’ mindset since he walked into the Oval Office on Jan 20.”

There are signs of a change in tone towards Trump across the media landscape.

The president has been mentioned about 400,000 times by major news outlets since he took office a year ago, according to figures compiled for The Telegraph by market research firm Carma, with 75pc of those either neutral or positive.

It helps that the president is so willing to talk. Trump took questions from journalists on 449 occasions in roughly his first 300 days in the office, according to an analysis from The New York Times.

That compares to 223 during the same period of his first term. On average, that’s two interactions with journalists a day – double his rate compared to 2017.

For many, Trump’s efforts to flood the zone have one purpose: to distract from scandals or uncomfortable lines of questioning – not least on the question of the Epstein files. By providing a constant stream of provocation, critics say, Trump hopes that bad news will simply be buried in an increasingly febrile news cycle.

At the same time, Trump is addressing his supporters directly via Truth Social, where he vacillates between policy announcements and meandering rants in block capitals.

Meanwhile, acolytes are spreading his message on other social media platforms such as Meta and X, where disinformation has flourished since the relaxation of fact-checking policies.

And while Trump’s first term in office triggered a so-called “Trump bump”, as more Americans signed up for news subscriptions, the same effect has not been felt the second time around.

Fox News gained audience share when Trump returned to the White House last January, securing the highest reach of any media outlet in the US with 32pc of the market, according to research by the Reuters Institute. CNN, by contrast, has not benefited, with many progressives pulling back from news media entirely.

Meanwhile, trust in US media has been eroded, falling from 38pc to just 26pc over the course of Trump’s first administration, ranking the country bottom among 48 global markets.

By undermining the media and overwhelming the news cycle, Trump may hope to emerge as the loudest voice in a sea of confusion.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: The Independent to run The Standard
data.description:
The Independent will take over control of running The Standard as Lord Lebedev wields the axe across his media empire.

The London newspaper, formerly known as The Evening Standard, is in negotiations over a deal that would see the Independent take control of its website and commercial operations.

The Standard, which shut down its daily print operations and moved to a weekly edition around 16 months ago, will maintain a “core” print team thought to be around 25 people.

In an email to staff on Tuesday, Tamar Riley, the chief executive, said the print edition would be “supported by a cost structure that future-proofs the business”.

The newspaper employs 75 people across its commercial and editorial divisions. While some journalists are expected to transfer to the Independent, the shake-up puts dozens of jobs at risk.

Bosses have given staff less than a week to apply for voluntary redundancy, which is capped at £22,000 plus a £3,000 bonus. Those who refuse to transfer will have their contracts terminated on March 1.

Journalists at the newspaper said they had been blindsided by the announcement, which underscores The Standard’s struggle for direction following a painful rebirth in late 2024.

The rise of smartphones, flexible working and Wi-Fi on the Tube all accelerated a fall in readership for The Standard, fuelling heavy losses and prompting the shutdown of the daily newspaper.

However, critics have taken aim at the editorial output of the new weekly print edition, which is focused on arts and culture rather than London news. One insider branded it a “highbrow magazine in grubby newsprint”.

Meanwhile, the publisher’s website has been reduced to a skeleton team, with output focused on celebrity news optimised for social media.

The Independent, which moved to an online-only edition a decade ago, is also focused on click-friendly stories, though the company has enjoyed more commercial success.

The Independent grew its revenues by 15pc to over £50m in 2024, thanks largely to major expansion in the US, where it is now the fifth largest newspaper title. It has also expanded in the UK by taking control of BuzzFeed and HuffPost.

Lord Lebedev has overseen The Standard since his father, Alexander, a sanctioned oligarch and former KGB agent, bought it in 2009.

The plans suggest that Lord Lebedev is seeking further cost savings to keep the London newspaper running.

The Standard has lost a total of £125m over the last eight years and the tycoon has poured in £30m over recent years to keep it afloat.

Those inside the newspaper describe it as “chaotic and shambolic” since the relaunch, which itself led to around 150 job losses, including at least 70 cuts in editorial, slashing the newsroom by more than half.

The Standard has also been without a permanent editor since the overhaul, when Dylan Jones stepped down after just 18 months in charge.

Sources close to the company said cost-cutting would help to reduce losses sharply and put the business on a course to break even.

Lord Lebedev owns The Standard alongside Sultan Mohamed Abuljadayel, the Saudi businessman who took a 30pc stake in the newspaper in 2018. He is also the largest shareholder in The Independent, alongside Sultan Abuljadayel and media entrepreneur Justin Byam Shaw.

data.title: Diesel lorries to be banned in eco drive
data.description:
Labour has unveiled plans to introduce a petrol and diesel lorry ban as part of their net-zero drive.

Ministers have announced an end to the sale of new fossil fuel-powered trucks in a move that opens up a new battle with the Tories and Reform.

They have ruled out allowing the continued use of low-carbon or synthetic fuels, meaning that from 2040 all new heavy goods vehicles (HGVs) will have to be electric.

Critics warned that forcing haulage companies to adopt expensive battery or hydrogen-powered vehicles would push up the cost of business.

The extra expense would then be passed onto consumers in the form of higher prices for goods like food and clothes, as well as parcel deliveries.

Richard Holden, the Tory shadow transport secretary, said: “Labour are sleepwalking into a cost shock for the entire economy.

“Forcing haulage firms to scrap perfectly good lorries while they can still run for years and replace them with other vehicles before either the technology or infrastructure is ready will simply drive up costs, as well as being environmentally disastrous.

“Those costs will be passed straight through supply chains into higher prices in shops, higher construction costs, and more pressure on inflation. Every supermarket shelf, building site, and home delivery will feel higher prices.

“Rather than letting innovation and the market cut emissions sensibly, Labour are reaching straight for quotas, caps and bans. Central planning with a delivery charge attached is delivering another blow to the British economy.”

The move risks exposing Labour to further attacks from the Tories and Reform, who have both pledged to roll back costly net zero policies.

Both parties have said they would scrap the 2030 petrol car ban and cut subsidies, which would cost taxpayers billions a year.

The 2040 date for phasing out petrol trucks was first proposed by the Tories in November 2021 at the height of Boris Johnson’s net zero ambitions.

In a consultation document published last week, the Department for Transport set out three options for enforcing the new petrol lorry ban.

The first would see manufacturers set annual quotas for the number of electric HGVs they must sell, similar to how the petrol car ban will work.

Another option laid out by ministers is to follow the EU’s approach and set haulage companies ever decreasing carbon emissions caps.

Finally, delivery firms could be required to ensure that an ever increasing proportion of their fleet was electric, eventually reaching 100 per cent.

Chris Ashley, from the Road Haulage Association (RHA), said: “We’re clear that the decarbonisation transition hinges on two key issues being addressed – infrastructure in place to power zero emission HGVs, and vehicle affordability.

“The pathway to decarbonise lorries and coaches in the UK must be realistic and viable – we’ll look to see how the consultation achieves that.”

In a survey run by the RHA last year, seven in 10 haulage firms said they had no plans to add any zero-emission vehicles to their fleets. The main barriers cited were the extra costs involved in buying electric-powered lorries and concerns over their lack of range.

Figures from February last year show that there were just 500 electric HGVs registered in the UK out of a total fleet of 535,000.

Extra subsidies needed

Separately, the Green Finance Institute has estimated £100bn worth of additional finance and subsidies would be needed to support electric lorry take-up.

More than 80 per cent of goods are transported around Britain by road, meaning an increase in cost for hauliers could have a significant impact on prices.

The logistics sector also employs almost 2.7 million workers, with its more than 200,000 businesses adding £185bn to the UK economy every year.

For smaller trucks up to 26 tonnes, the sale of new petrol and diesel-powered models will be banned from as early as 2035 under the plans.

Keir Mather, the minister for transport decarbonisation, said the Government was aware it would be “challenging” to decarbonise the sector. But he insisted there was a need to act because lorries “have a high environmental impact” and emit 16 per cent of all emissions from domestic transport.

“That is why we are firmly committed to accelerating the transition to clean technologies and to setting a path to phase out the sale of…all new non-zero emission heavy goods vehicles by 2040,” he wrote.

He claimed technological advances would lead to “ever better, cheaper and more capable vehicles” that would ultimately bring down costs for haulage firms.
