data.title: Netflix sweetens £62bn offer for Warner Bros in Hollywood bid battle
data.description:
Netflix has sweetened its $83bn (£62bn) takeover bid for Warner Bros after tabling a new cash-only offer to fend off rival Hollywood bidder Paramount.

The streaming giant on Tuesday said it would offer $27.75 per share in cash. Shareholders had previously been offered $23.25 in cash and $4.50 of Netflix shares, a deal Paramount had labelled too “complex”.

Netflix and Paramount, run by David Ellison, son of Oracle founder Larry Ellison, have been going head-to-head for control of Warner Bros for months in an increasingly bitter battle between the Hollywood giants.

Netflix said the updated bid, which was unanimously approved by the Warner Bros board, would provide greater certainty for shareholders and accelerate the takeover. The company added that it expects investors to vote on the deal by April.

It came as the streaming giant issued a cautious outlook for profits this year because of an increase in spending on programmes and costs associated with the Warner Bros deal. 

Shares fell as much as 5.1pc in after hours trading in New York because of the expected weaker profits. 

Netflix said it intends to boost spending on TV shows and films by 10pc in 2026 while buying Warner Bros will add $275m in extra costs this year. 

The expected cost of the acquisition has led management to pause share buybacks to save more cash.

The US company is trying to shore up support from shareholders amid growing pressure from Paramount, which is attempting to gatecrash the deal with its own $108bn takeover bid.

Paramount has previously criticised Netflix’s bid as overly complex and warned that the value to shareholders was dependent on fluctuations in the streaming giant’s share price.

The studio has also criticised a lack of clarity over how Warner Bros values its traditional cable business, which owns networks such as CNN.

Under the terms of the Netflix deal, Warner Bros will spin off the cable business into a separate company called Discovery Global prior to the deal taking place. By contrast, Paramount has tabled a bid for the entire group.

In a stock market filing on Tuesday, Warner Bros said its advisers had valued Discovery Global at a broad range between 72¢ and $1.65 per share. It said shareholders would receive this additional value once the spin-off had been completed.

Warner Bros also said Discovery Global’s debt would be reduced by $260m thanks to improved cash flow. As a result, the division is expected to have net debt of $17bn, falling to $16.1bn by the end of the year.

Netflix’s all-cash bid will increase pressure on Paramount to improve its own approach. The company has previously sweetened its bid by saying Larry Ellison would provide a personal guarantee for $40bn in equity, but has not increased the value of the offer.

Paramount argues that a merger between Netflix and Warner Bros would pose significant competition risks as it would lead to the merger of two major streaming services – Netflix and HBO Max.

It has also warned of a risk to cinemas, arguing that Netflix could reduce the number of films released theatrically.

Mr Ellison last week met Culture Secretary Lisa Nandy as the company ramps up its lobbying efforts across Europe. Senior executives have also held meetings with Ofcom and the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA).

Netflix has played down competition concerns and insisted it is committed to releasing films in cinemas. Nevertheless, it has acknowledged that regulatory reviews could take up to 18 months.

David Zaslav, the president and chief executive of Warner Bros Discovery, said: “Today’s revised merger agreement brings us even closer to combining two of the greatest storytelling companies in the world, and with it, even more people enjoying the entertainment they love to watch the most.

“By coming together with Netflix, we will combine the stories Warner Bros has told that have captured the world’s attention for more than a century and ensure audiences continue to enjoy them for generations to come.”

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Britain’s busiest station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming East London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station will ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on the them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Goldman Sachs traders eye push into gambling on prediction markets
data.description:
Goldman Sachs traders are eyeing a push into the world of gambling on prediction markets.

David Solomon, the bank’s chief executive, said he had met with the bosses of major prediction markets in recent weeks and wanted to understand the opportunities on offer.

Traders are eyeing a push into the space after an explosion in their popularity over the last year.

Prediction markets allow individuals to bet on the outcomes of events as diverse as whether the US would bomb Iran to who would win the Golden Globes. The two largest markets in the US are Kalshi and Polymarket, which accepts bets in cryptocurrency.

Mr Solomon said: “I personally met with the two big prediction companies and their leadership in the last two weeks and spent a couple of hours with each to learn more about that. 

“We have a team of people here that are spending time with them and are looking at it.”

Polymarket was banned from operating in the US under the Biden administration, however, Donald Trump has lifted restrictions as part of a broader embrace of the crypto market.

The platform rose to prominence after users correctly predicted Mr Trump’s resounding win in the 2024 election, despite pollsters predicting a much tighter race.

Kalshi, a similar platform, was created by Tarek Mansour, who came up with the concept while working as an equity derivatives intern at Goldman Sachs in 2016.

At the time, institutions were scrambling to guard against the fallout from Brexit, giving Mr Mansour the idea of an exchange to trade events themselves rather than financial assets.

While these markets effectively amount to gambling on the outcome of world events, they are considered to be financial derivative products in the US and are regulated in the same way as futures or options contracts that Goldman and other banks already trade.

‘Opportunity’ to scale

Mr Solomon’s comments came as Goldman Sachs retook its crown as the best bank on Wall Street for stock trading after a record $4.3bn (£3.2bn) haul for the final three months of last year.

The profit from trading was almost $700m higher than what analysts had expected. It ended Morgan Stanley’s three-month spell as the top stock trading bank on Wall Street, a spot that Goldman had previously held since the end of 2022.

On a busy day for results in Wall Street, Goldman and its rival Morgan Stanley both reported a rise in profits in the fourth quarter.

Mr Solomon added that corporate bosses are trying to use the next three years of the Trump administration to pursue major deals.

He said: “I think CEOs and boards are looking and saying, OK, we’ve got a window here of a handful of years where the opportunity to consider big, strategic, transformative things is certainly possible.

“CEOs definitely believe that the art of the deal and scaled consolidation is possible now.”

BlackRock, the world’s biggest asset manager, also broke records on Thursday as it reported assets under management of over $14tn for the first time.

It pulled in $342bn of new client money, beating Wall Street estimates. Of this, $12.7bn came from private markets.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: How the 50/30/20 budgeting hack can unlock your finances
data.description:
Budgeting is the key to running your finances smoothly. Whether you have a high or low income – or somewhere in between – a budget is important to maintain the levels of spending and saving you can afford.

Getting a handle on your income and outgoings can help reset your finances and spending patterns by assessing where your money goes every month.

A budget can also be a real help in achieving specific financial goals you might have. This could be saving for a summer holiday or a new car, or raising a deposit for a house, a wedding, or even paying off debts.

There are many different budgeting methods to choose from.

The 50/30/20 rule is a straightforward budgeting method that can help you to manage your money simply and effectively so you live within your means and reach your goals – whether that’s to spend less to pay off debts or to save more for your future.

How the 50/30/20 rule works

This method of budgeting suggests dividing your monthly income into three spending categories: 50pc for “needs”, 30pc for “wants” and 20pc for “financial goals”.

The 50pc towards needs will be the portion that covers all the regular essential outgoings such as your mortgage or rent, and household bills such as gas and electricity, water, mobile phone, house insurance, as well as the cost of running a car if you have one.

You would then set aside 30pc for wants, which pays for things like hobbies and sports, socialising, shopping and subscriptions for TV, music streaming or newspapers and magazines.

The remaining 20pc can be used for financial goals, such as paying off debt, contributing to savings, making longer-term investments or saving into a pension.

The 50/30/20 rule can work, in theory, whatever your income level.

Someone earning £34,000 (around the UK national average) would take home £28,000 a year or £2,333 a month, according to calculations by Hargreaves Lansdown, an investment platform.

According to the 50/30/20 rule, this would give you £1,166 a month to spend on needs, £700 on wants, and £467 on savings. With a 5pc pension contribution – which is £142 a month – that would leave you £325 to put aside in the savings pot.

A higher earner on a salary of £60,000 would take home £46,357 a year, or £3,780 a month.

It would give you £1,890 a month to spend on needs, £1,134 on wants and £756 on savings. A 5pc pension contribution – which is £250 a month – means you’re left with £506 to save or repay debts.

If you had a credit card debt of £5,000, you would be able to clear it between 13 and 16 months if you used the budgeting rule.

Equally, this amount could go towards your £20,000 annual tax-free Isa allowance.

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “How you use the savings money will depend on your circumstances.

“Some will need to pay down expensive short-term debts, while others will want to build emergency savings, their pension or investments – often people will want to do all of these things, so will split the cash between the different goals.”

Bending the budgeting rules

One big problem with this method is that it may not be possible to get all your essential bills into the 50pc bracket.

Ms Coles said: “The proportions won’t work for everyone, largely because the cost of putting a roof over your head is so high in the UK.”

Ms Coles calculated that if you took a middle-income household that earns £32,651 after tax – or £2,721 a month – the cost of their essentials is £1,755 a month, which is 65pc of their income.

If you then put 30pc aside for wants, you’re left with next to nothing to save for the future.

It means you need to flex your “wants” to fit the cash available, and free up enough money for the future, which might work out as 65pc needs, 15pc wants and 20pc savings.

To cut the cost of your “wants”, you’ll need to come up with some sacrifices.

Cutting out one of the restaurant meals you have each month can mean money is redirected to the needs pot, for example. You might also need to ruthlessly cut shopping trips and subscriptions to achieve your goals.

If that doesn’t work for you, you may find that other breakdowns are more achievable – and these could change during the year as life dictates different spending patterns.

Ms Coles added: “Simple rules of thumb like the 50/30/20 can be useful for keeping you roughly on track. However, it’s not a good idea to try to follow them without calculating whether they’ll work for you, or they could lead you astray.”

Timeless budgeting tips

Sticking to a budget can be tricky, and if you’ve already tried a method that didn’t work for you it can be easy to give up.

Budgeting isn’t a one-size-fits-all situation. If you don’t think you can stick to such a strict regime such as the 50/30/20 rule, some of these key budgeting tips can help you stay in control of your money.

1. Understand where your money goes

You might roughly know what you spend your money on each month, but it will help to know your exact outgoings – and what they’re spent on.

If you don’t review your spending often, one way to do this is to go through your bank statements either online or on a banking app and make a list of the bills you pay as well as amounts for online subscriptions, eating out, travel, supermarket shopping, clothes, sports and other treats.

The list might be long, but you can then see where your earnings go – and where any savings can be made.

Many banking apps will automatically categorise your spending so you can easily see whether a large proportion of your money goes on eating out.

If yours doesn’t, then apps such as Moneyhub, Snoop and Emma can do it for you.

2. Know your goals

Each of us has different goals and things we want to save up for – whether it’s a holiday, home improvements or a wedding – or debts to pay off.

Keeping these in mind when thinking about your spending and saving means you have a better chance of reaching them.

3. ‘No spend’ weekends

When an unexpected expense comes your way, such as a car repair or faulty boiler, you may feel the pinch.

A no-spend weekend can be a great way to offset any overspending you’ve done. It involves going 48 hours without tapping any card or buying online. It’s not necessarily about giving up things you enjoy doing, but rather an opportunity to get creative with ways to have fun for free.

This can also be a way of preparing for an expense, boosting your savings.

For an even bigger challenge, you could join the people making “no spend” months popular.

4. Be open about your money mission

Tell friends and family if you’re on a strict money diet so they can support you.

This tactic – also known as “loud budgeting” – can hopefully mean you won’t be embarrassed to opt out of expensive dinners out and any other pricey get-togethers, as you’ll have already made it clear that you’re keeping your spending to a minimum.

5. Seek help if you need it

Some restructuring of your borrowing might be enough to help you regain control.

But if this and some smart budgeting aren’t enough to resolve your money problems, then don’t shy away from seeking help.

Debt advice is best sought from a debt charity, which will advise you, and act on your behalf, with nothing to pay for their services.

Help from a debt charity will have an immediate benefit as they can often negotiate freezing interest payments on some debts to give you some breathing space while you work out a plan for the road ahead.

Beware of debt help or loan consolidation companies that market their services heavily. They will often charge for advice and these fees will only serve to add to your existing debts.

Try the National Debtline (0808 808 4000), StepChange Debt Charity (0800 138 1111), or the Debt Advice Foundation (0800 043 40 50).

data.title: Black Sheep Brewery rescued by Yorkshire rival in £6.5m deal
data.description:
The brewery behind some of Britain’s best-known craft beers has been rescued by a rival drinks company in a £6.5m deal.

Yorkshire-based Black Sheep Brewery has avoided collapse after being taken over by Paramount Retail Group, the owner of Bradford’s Saltaire Brewery.

The acquisition was struck two months after Black Sheep’s parent company filed a notice of its intent to appoint administrators.

Paramount Retail Group, a family-owned conglomerate which also owns several pet food suppliers, has agreed to invest £2m into Black Sheep as part of the deal.

It is expected to save 145 jobs and safeguard the future of its brewery.

A new company called the Great British Drinks Company was created to complete the rescue, which was carried out as part of a pre-pack administration process.

It brings an end to a turbulent period for Black Sheep, which has warned about the threat of administration four times since the pandemic.

The company was sold in 2023 to London investment firm Breal Capital for £5m in a deal that wiped out 1,000 investors.

Breal went on to buy several other beer brands before merging them all under one umbrella, the Keystone Brewing Group.

Black Sheep recorded a £3.1m loss in 2024 after being hit by high inflation, inflated borrowing costs and weak demand.

At the time, the brewery had debts exceeding £6m – including to HMRC.

Craft beer surged in popularity throughout the 2010s as a slew of new breweries sprung up across the country. In recent years, many of these start-up brewers have been snapped up by global beer giants eager to cash in.

Camden Town Brewery was bought by AB InBev, the maker of Stella Artois, in 2015 for £85m. Heineken has control of both Beavertown and Brixton Breweries.

Many of the breweries that have remained independent have struggled in the wake of the cost of living crisis, with drinkers less willing to fork out for an expensive craft pint. The number of breweries entering insolvency jumped by 82pc in 2023, according to Maazars, an audit company.

Brewery woes come amid wider stress on the pub and hospitality sector. Mounting taxes on pubs have driven up the cost of an average pint of lager to £4.82, with some in London charging over £8.

Pubs’ business rates are expected to jump by 76pc on average over three years as a result of tax rises in Labour’s last Budget, according to trade body UK Hospitality.

Black Sheep Brewery rescued by Yorkshire rival in £6.5m deal

The Telegraph is campaigning to save Britain’s pubs by tackling high taxes and energy costs.

Black Sheep, which was founded in 1992 by a member of the Theakston brewing family, has continued to trade despite the threat of collapse.

In late November last year, the company said: “We’re still here, and we’re still fighting for the future of great beer.

“To our customers, partners and supporters, we want to say thank you for standing with us; your continued loyalty means everything to our team and we’re determined to come through this stronger than ever.”

Ravi Sharma, of Great British Drinks Company, said: “This isn’t just a restoration deal, it’s about restoring pride to Yorkshire. The region has a proud brewing heritage, and we could not stand by and let that be lost.

“The next few months will be exciting, the business has some fantastic people, brilliant customers and a group of suppliers that we will be very focused on rebuilding relationships with.”

data.title: ‘Un-pensioning’ could set a very dangerous precedent indeed
data.description:
Join Michael in the comment section below from 11am.

At least 150 teachers have been banned from schools over the last 10 years for child sexual abuse.

The true figure could be considerably higher as Freedom of Information Act requests show that since 2015 a further 256 were struck off for non-defined sexual misconduct and another 245 for “breach of boundaries/trust”.

Yet all these nearly 700 former teachers – indeed all 1,095 teachers who have been disbarred for whatever reason since 2015 – have retained whatever pension entitlements they previously accrued.

The power exists to strip teachers of their pension if they commit treason (thankfully still not the most common of occurrences), serious violations of the Official Secrets Act (there will be little in the way of classified information lying around most schools) or offences committed in connection with their role that could damage confidence in the profession or are “gravely injurious to the state”.

But this power has not been exercised in recent decades. No teacher has lost their pension since at least 2005. The power is also rarely used in relation to others in the public sector – under the NHS pension scheme just 33 people have been stripped of their entitlements since the 1970s.

Now, victims groups are calling for this to change and for those who have so despicably breached the trust placed in them to lose their pensions.

One must have sympathy with this line of argument.

Few sane people would wish to stand up for the rights of paedophiles. Those who abuse children in their care fully deserve to be punished to the full extent the law permits.

There is a very strong case for awarding the pensions of abusers to their victims. Such actions are not just criminal, they are also civil wrongs for which victims are entitled to sue for compensation and seize assets from their abusers.

But in terms of the state stripping people of their pension entitlements, let us be careful what we wish for.

This is a dangerous road to go down, especially in the hands of our current Labour Government and the woke educational and NHS blob. What starts with a clampdown on sex abusers could soon end up with public sector workers losing their pensions for wrong-speak. The legions of the debanked – almost half a million customers last year – could then be joined by the unpensioned.

This is not an argument about the sustainability of public sector pensions and whether the current arrangements can be justified. The Teachers’ Pension Scheme costs taxpayers around £9bn per year. The total liability of public sector pensions is estimated at £5.8tn.

If all these schemes were closed now, annual costs would still rise for decades – peaking at £132bn in 2061.

The whole system is in urgent need of reform, but that is a completely different issue from individuals losing their entitlements for misconduct. In addition, that public sector pensions are such a costly behemoth is not the fault of individual teachers, nurses or doctors. It is a consequence of decades of cowardice by politicians of all parties, finding it easier to avoid having to take difficult decisions.

The reason why unpensioning miscreants is generally wrong, I would argue, is that don’t imagine if ministers start to get a taste for such action they will stop with such offenders. Again and again, policies which have started as a means of combatting a genuine issue have morphed into a much wider bureaucratic power grab.

Just take a look at the Prevent programme at schools, set up to combat Islamist extremism. Examining the scheme now, you might imagine that the biggest domestic threat to British national security did not come from Islamic State adherents and their fellow travellers, but from the extreme Right.

In the year to the end of March 2024, 45pc of referrals were for “extreme Right concerns”, almost double the 23pc referred for “Islamist concerns”. Another 18pc were referred for the rather catch all-sounding “conflicted ideology”.

The case against unpensioning people we wholly disapprove of is the same as why we should champion free speech for those with views we abhor.

Standing up for the rights of people we broadly agree with to express their views is simple. The real test is whether we are willing to stand up for the rights of those whose views we hate.

It is the easiest thing in the world for someone of my world view to be outraged if a university bans a speaker for their Brexit-y, gender critical or pro-Israel views. It is much more difficult – and for that reason all the more important – to stand up for freedom of expression for trans activists and those taking, say, a virulent anti-Israel stance.

If we clamour for the unpensioning of those who are genuinely reprehensible, we will have handed another tool to the Whitehall machine to regulate behaviour they don’t approve of. And who knows who will be next in their sights.

By past performance, it could well be those expressing views which run counter to the prevailing consensus. And those views will generally be of a conservative persuasion.

data.title: Fund giant to offload £1.9bn of UK shares
data.description:
One of the world’s biggest money managers is reducing its investments in Britain after UK customers said they wanted more of their cash to go overseas.

Vanguard, which looks after around £9tn of client money, said it would sell around £1.9bn worth of UK stocks held by its LifeStrategy funds, which together own £52bn of assets around the world.

The company plans to reduce the “home bias” of funds in the LifeStrategy UK range, which governs how much money is invested in local markets.

It means that LifeStrategy funds will cut their exposure to UK equities to 20pc, down from 25pc currently. For bond funds, the allocation is being cut even further from 35pc to 20pc.

The US company said its decision to cut back exposure to Britain reflected the fact that UK investors “express a clear preference for international diversification”.

The move is embarrassing for Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, who has been seeking to boost London’s stock market and this week hailed the start of a “new golden age” for the exchange.

Henry Norton, senior investment manager at private bank Arbuthnot Latham, said Vanguard’s move “will be a blow to the Government at a time when they are trying to convince investors of the benefits of increasing their exposure to the UK”.

LifeStrategy is a group of funds launched in 2011 that spread investment across cash, bonds and stocks globally. The exact mix is tailored for each fund to meet people’s life goals – such as delivering a steady stream of income, growing wealth or protecting it.

The changes will gradually be rolled out between March and June this year.

UK investors look overseas

In a note to clients, Vanguard said British investors “have grown more confident investing internationally” and its LifeStrategy funds had consequently “evolved to have a more global focus.”

“Today, our UK adviser clients express a clear preference for international diversification alongside a strong commitment to their home market,” it said.

The decision comes despite UK stocks outperforming Wall Street last year amid the turmoil created by Donald Trump’s tariff campaign and concerns about an AI bubble.

The FTSE 100 rose by 21.5pc in 2025 compared with 16.4pc for the benchmark S&P 500 in the US.

The Chancellor has pressured asset managers and pension funds to invest more in the UK stock market. Pension funds agreed to invest at least 5pc of their assets into UK private markets under Ms Reeves’s Mansion House Accord last year.

Vanguard’s shift away from the City comes days after the Chancellor hailed new rules designed to make it easier and cheaper for companies to list in London. She claimed the changes made Britain “the best place in the world to start, scale and list a company”.

The asset manager had also backed the Chancellor’s retail investment campaign set to launch in April, which is designed to encourage more people to put more into the stock market.

Michael Brown, an analyst at Pepperstone, said Vanguard’s decision was “in keeping with the rest of the industry”.

He added: “Having said that, the optics of this certainly aren’t great for the Chancellor and it will undoubtedly be seen as a sign of investors further losing faith in the London market and in Reeves’s ideas to revive things.”

The Chancellor has toyed with the idea of forcing pension funds to invest a minimum level in UK assets, though the industry has pushed back strongly against this.

Vanguard said it “regularly” reviews its fund allocations but added that the “optimal allocation to UK shares and bonds to achieve each fund’s objective has changed”.

So far this year, the FTSE 100 has surpassed the 10,000 mark for the first time and has closed at a record high seven times.

‘A lot of angst’

Bosses have become more frustrated with Labour.

Rishi Khosla, the chief executive of challenger bank Oaknorth, said Labour’s tax raids and raft of new employment laws did not match its pro-business rhetoric before the 2024 election.

Speaking on the sidelines of the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, Mr Khosla said:

“Things like the workers rights bill, business rates for any business that is consumer facing, are things that are biting. They’re biting this year, and therefore there’s a lot of angst.”

Also speaking in Davos, the boss of drugs giant Novartis said Labour had not made the UK competitive enough to attract new investment and vital drugs.

While Mr Narasimhan praised the Government for taking “important steps” towards addressing a drug pricing row, he said the amount the NHS was willing to pay for new drugs remained “on par with Eastern Europe”.

Mr Narasimhan said: “If the UK really wants to make biotech a highly competitive sector, it has to start valuing innovation in the way the leading countries in the world do.”

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, claimed the industry was “front and centre” of Britain’s growth plan, saying that investment was starting to flow into the country following a series of threats to pull money out of the UK.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”
